q3: The middle paragraph in the significance section initially undersold the difficulty of the research problem — it made the sensor data sound like a solved retrieval problem rather than a genuine scientific challenge. I had to name that explicitly before it got fixed, which means the model didn't catch it on its own the first time.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about — the narrative arc criterion especially maps closely to how I think about significance sections. But it felt a little abstract in places; 'earned authority' is the right idea but the rubric didn't quite capture my specific instinct about pilot data framing, which is that over-presenting what's done makes reviewers question what's left to fund. The model got there eventually but I'm not sure the rubric alone would have driven it there.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is actually quite good at narrative structure when prompted toward it — the opening and closing of the significance section were strong from early on, which tracks with the 'narrative arc' criterion being listed first. What it surfaced as a gap is that the model can accidentally make hard problems sound easy when it's trying to sound compelling, and the rubric's 'earned authority' criterion doesn't quite name that failure mode precisely enough to prevent it.

q3: The model had one real stumble — it claimed a response wasn't cut off when it clearly was, and then reconstructed options that may or may not have matched what was originally generated. That kind of confident inaccuracy is a problem in a professional context. The prose itself was strong, but that moment of false certainty was unsettling.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and honestly it captured more of what I care about than I expected — the narrative arc criterion and the cross-disciplinary accessibility criterion in particular felt like they were written for exactly the kind of work I do. What it didn't fully capture was my instinct about how pilot data should be framed as feasibility rather than accomplishment; the rubric gestures at that under 'Strategic Framing' but doesn't quite articulate the distinction the way I'd put it.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently strong on narrative momentum — it genuinely understood that the significance section needs to build pressure, not catalogue facts — but the rubric also made me notice that the model's weakest area was the earned authority criterion. A few claims in the draft felt asserted rather than substantiated, and I might not have flagged that as a pattern without the rubric making me look for it explicitly.

q3: The middle section kept defaulting to an accomplishment-showcase mode — framing our cohort and prior work as things we'd done rather than conditions that had finally made the question answerable. It took several rounds to get that framing right, and even then I had to name the distinction explicitly before it clicked.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, and the 'Strategic Framing of Preliminary Work' criterion in particular is exactly the distinction I spend the most time fighting for in real proposals. What it doesn't quite capture is the emotional arc I want in a significance section — the rubric describes it correctly but the model still sometimes executed it as a checklist rather than a felt experience. The rubric was useful scaffolding but not a substitute for the back-and-forth.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on the opening and closing of a section than on the connective tissue in the middle — it could nail a first sentence and a last sentence but the middle paragraphs sometimes lost the thread and defaulted to informational rather than narrative mode. I wouldn't have articulated that pattern as clearly without the rubric giving me a framework to compare against.
